coredata.dc:title: A practical solution for the state of charge estimation in lithium-ion batteries considering disturbance and errors in the measurement sensors
coredata.dc:creator: Rezaei, Omid
Menhaj, Mohammad Bagher
Sharifi, Iman
Khosravi, Mohammad A.
coredata.dc:description: In lithium-ion battery management systems (BMS), state of charge (SoC) is a key parameter that is used in most other functions. This paper introduces a different approach from the past presented methods and tackles the problems of model uncertainties, stochastic disturbances, measurement errors, and nonlinearity problems with an adaptive Hꝏ unknown input observer (AHUIO). In the proposed algorithm, model uncertainties are considered as unknown terms in the battery model, the effect of which is removed by a UIO. Furthermore, the effect of stochastic disturbances during the measurement and the battery operation is eliminated using the Hꝏ mechanism. Besides, an adaptive law guarantees asymptotic stability as well as sensor error estimation. In this way, the battery SoC is estimated with no sensor error effects. More specifically, during the stability proof, a special stability condition is considered, and using an adaptive law, the problem is reformed into a linear matrix inequality (LMI) problem. Finally, in solving the LMI problem utilizing the YALMIP toolbox, the estimator parameters are designed in such a way the asymptotic stability and Hꝏ criteria are guaranteed. Using experimental tests for 2.4 Ah fresh and aged lithium battery, the effectiveness of the AHUIO is validated, and the results confirm that the AHUIO algorithm has an estimation error of 0.2 % and 0.42 % for both cells. Besides, the effect of sensor errors on the SoC estimation has been investigated, and the results confirmed the superiority of the proposed method in sensor error elimination in contrast with the other existing methods.

row.entry: The time complexity of this estimator depends on the degree of nonlinearity of the system as well as the degree of the system.
This estimator needs a real-time sliding surface to reach convergence, which adds complexity to nonlinear systems.
This observer has a moderate computational load but is sensitive to chattering and requires parameter tuning to increase its robustness.
The time complexity of this filter depends on the dimensions of the state vector, which is directly related to the number of estimated parameters.
Uses computational matrices, which can increase the computational load, especially on systems with a high number of state variables.

For linear systems, these filters are easy to implement, but for nonlinear systems, their implementation is more complicated, and it is also a little difficult to adjust the measurement and process noise covariance matrices.
The time complexity of these methods is highly dependent on the number of rules and state variables.

These methods use fuzzy membership functions and also use rules for their calculations. Defuzzification and fuzzification steps are the most important steps of these methods, which increases their complexity.
If the number of rules set for this system is small, it is easier to implement, but as the number of rules increases, the complexity of implementation increases.
The time complexity of these methods depends on the number of inputs and the number of neurons, and as these two increase, the time complexity increases.
When training these networks, forward and backward propagation are required, which increases the amount of computation.
The heavy training intervals and their dependence on hyperparameters make it difficult to implement and tune.
The time complexity of these methods depends on solving linear matrix inequalities.
This method involves solving some optimization problems and matrix calculations, which makes the calculations heavy.
Due to the heavy computational volume, the implementation of the methods is difficult and requires strong processors.
The time complexity of this method depends on simple matrix calculations, and compared to other methods, it has a low time complexity.
In this method, an LMI problem is solved offline, and a series of parameters is extracted. Finally, according to Eqs. (14) and (31), the estimation is done using simple matrix calculations.
Due to the moderate computational load, the implementation of this method is simple compared to methods such as the Kalman family.

head.ce:title: A practical solution for the state of charge estimation in lithium-ion batteries considering disturbance and errors in the measurement sensors

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Electrical Engineering, Amirkabir University of Technology (Tehran Polytechnic), Tehran, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Electrical Engineering, Amirkabir University of Technology, Tehran, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In lithium-ion battery management systems (BMS), state of charge (SoC) is a key parameter that is used in most other functions. This paper introduces a different approach from the past presented methods and tackles the problems of model uncertainties, stochastic disturbances, measurement errors, and nonlinearity problems with an adaptive Hꝏ unknown input observer (AHUIO). In the proposed algorithm, model uncertainties are considered as unknown terms in the battery model, the effect of which is removed by a UIO. Furthermore, the effect of stochastic disturbances during the measurement and the battery operation is eliminated using the Hꝏ mechanism. Besides, an adaptive law guarantees asymptotic stability as well as sensor error estimation. In this way, the battery SoC is estimated with no sensor error effects. More specifically, during the stability proof, a special stability condition is considered, and using an adaptive law, the problem is reformed into a linear matrix inequality (LMI) problem. Finally, in solving the LMI problem utilizing the YALMIP toolbox, the estimator parameters are designed in such a way the asymptotic stability and Hꝏ criteria are guaranteed. Using experimental tests for 2.4 Ah fresh and aged lithium battery, the effectiveness of the AHUIO is validated, and the results confirm that the AHUIO algorithm has an estimation error of 0.2 % and 0.42 % for both cells. Besides, the effect of sensor errors on the SoC estimation has been investigated, and the results confirmed the superiority of the proposed method in sensor error elimination in contrast with the other existing methods.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Nowadays, due to the phenomenon of global warming caused by fossil fuels and greenhouse gases, the use of renewable energy has been given attention [1]. This issue has caused the use of secondary batteries to increase day by day [2]. Lead-acid batteries are widely used in renewable energy systems due to their low-cost compared to other batteries [3]. But having disadvantages such as low energy density have made their use limited. Based on reference [4], Lithium-ion batteries are a suitable alternative to lead-acid batteries because of their advantages including fast charging, high energy density, reliability, low self-discharge rate, lightweight, and long life.

Accurate SoC estimation along with other functions like cell balancing, is an important challenge in the BMS, which ensures the correct operation of the battery [5]. Determining the SoC in a lithium-ion battery is complicated due to uncertainty in its characteristics such as nonlinearity. Direct measurement of SoC with any sensor is impossible and it must be estimated indirectly through voltage, current, temperature, etc. In this regard, research studies have introduced various methods to estimate SoC in recent years [6].

The most commonly used for SoC estimation include the impedance measurement method [7] and ampere-hour counting method [8]. The mentioned methods are simpler and cheaper compared to other SOC estimation methods. However, these methods are much less accurate than other methods and are highly sensitive and affected by environmental conditions [9].

The Kalman Filter (KF) is a common model-based technique for the SoC estimation. This filter, which includes recursive mathematical methods, is used to estimate the parameters of the system in the dynamic equations of the specific state space. The advantage of this filter is the ability to estimate the state variables in the presence of measurement noise. In other words, it can estimate the best state variables of the system by minimizing the mean squared error. For this reason, the KF is a conventional method for the battery state estimation [10]. The standard KF [11] is utilized to estimate SoC considering the linear model of the battery. But in reality, due to the battery having nonlinear dynamics, various types of nonlinear KFs, including the Unscented Kalman filter (UKF) [12] or the extended Kalman filter (EKF) [13], are introduced as alternatives to the standard Kalman.

Deng et al. introduced a robust EKF for SoC estimation including the non-Gaussian disturbances in the system, which reduces the computational complexity of SoC estimation [14]. Wang et al. introduced a dynamic matrix control in the EKF to update model parameters, thereby minimizing SoC estimation error [15]. Xiong et al. presented an adaptive fractional-order square root UKF that can prevent the divergence of the noise covariance matrix as well as real-time update of the noise information [16]. Wu et al. introduced a multi-scale fractional-order dual UKF to estimate the parameters and SoC in the case where the parameter estimation changes slowly and the SoC variation is rapid. As a result, the average of SoC estimation error remained in the range of 1 % [17]. As the EKF has a linearization process in its performance, it is less accurate, and also UKF requires a heavy computation. KFs need accurate dynamic models of batteries to estimate SoC, which reduces the estimation accuracy because of the uncertainties and environmental condition variation in the experimental test.

To solve the uncertainty problem in the model parameters of lithium-ion batteries, robust observers are introduced. For the SoC estimation, the most commonly used observers such as sliding mode observers [18] and Hꝏ observers [19] are utilized. Chen et al. presented a Hꝏ observer for SOC estimation, which can reduce the effects of uncertain noise and parameter disturbance [20]. Chen et al. designed an Hꝏ estimator based on the electrochemical model and accurately estimated the SoC. It also solves the problems appear due to the large changes in temperature and current during the actual operation of the battery packs [21].

On the other hand, the disadvantages of Hꝏ filters are that due to the high level of mathematical calculations and their complexity, they require very powerful processors, and as a result, the calculation time and the cost of its implementation increase. Also, the accuracy of estimation in sliding-mode-based observers decreases due to the chattering phenomenon in their working performance. To address solve the mentioned issue, various types of adaptive SMOs are used [22]. Nath et al. proposed a SMO to estimate SoC in real-time, which can estimate SoC quickly and optimally in the presence of model uncertainties, external disturbances, and system nonlinearity [23]. Xu et al. introduced an improved SMO that is able to accelerate convergence, limit the chattering phenomenon and improve SoC estimation accuracy with a novel adaptive law [24].

For this reason, to reduce the chattering phenomenon, complementary methods such as fuzzy systems [25] and neural networks (NN) [26] are used to make the main filter adaptive. Wei et al. introduced a dynamic neural network for the SoC estimation, which was improved by closed-loop nonlinear auto-regression models. In addition, they used the cosine-sine algorithm for optimization of the dynamic parameters in the proposed NN [27].

On the other hand, due to the presence of noise in measurement, these robust observers cannot correctly estimate the SoC of batteries. For the battery parameter estimation, methods such as intelligent systems and artificial intelligence (AI) [28] that include NNs [29] can be mentioned. Also, based on the review [30], fuzzy systems can be used directly or augmented to the other methods for the estimation of SoC. Yang et al. developed a fuzzy adaptive system with KF. By improving the convergence speed of the KF, this system can estimate SoC with high accuracy and the estimation error will be significantly reduced [31]. Fuzzy systems need to use optimization to increase accuracy and have low accuracy alone. On the other hand, to express fuzzy rules, high knowledge is needed for inference and reliability. In order to estimate SoC, recently, certain methods have been proposed by researchers, which are data-based methods [32], including deep learning [33], reinforcement learning [34], and machine learning [35].

Vellingiri et al. [33] a novel deep learning-based method used a hybrid convolutional NN and short-term and long-term memory model to accurately estimate the SoC. Barnacles optimizer was used for better estimation. Shrivastava. et al. presents a comprehensive estimation algorithm for the estimation of maximum available capacity, states, and maximum available energy in reference [36]. Rente et al. introduced a SoC estimation algorithm using sensor-based Fibre Bragg Grating. The SoC estimator with the dynamic time-warping algorithm and sensor data obtained results with an error of about 1 % [37]. One of the disadvantages of the mentioned methods is that they need a set of reliable data that are used to train the proposed intelligent systems. Also, powerful processors are needed to implement these methods.
As mentioned, there are various model-based methods for the SoC estimation in lithium-ion batteries. Each of these methods has its own weaknesses for the SoC estimation.

The Kalman filter family needs an accurate model of the battery, and consequently, model uncertainties, measurement errors, and disturbances impressively decrease their performance. As in practical operations, model uncertainties, measurement errors, and disturbances are undeniable, Kalman filters have weaknesses in their performance. Also, selecting the covariance matrices of process and measurement noises, which impressively affect the estimation performance, is not an easy task in practical implementations. In sliding mode observers, the chattering phenomena, limited robustness, and convergence time are the most important weaknesses. Besides, they cannot eliminate the effect of measurement errors on the SoC estimation. Fuzzy systems strongly depend on the knowledge of the person who selects the rules, and usually, these systems need an augmented method to have optimal performance. The main drawback of neural networks and learning systems is their need for a complete and reliable data set for training, which is not always available. Other robust methods like Hꝏ need a strong processor for their implementation, as the complexity is their main drawback. Also, large-scale uncertainties and measurement errors impressively decrease their performance.

To cover these drawbacks, this paper presents an AHUIO method which is able to: 1- Eliminate the effect of the measurement error on the SoC estimation. 2- Estimate the measurement error (sensor fault). 3- Resist model uncertainties. 4- Eliminate the effect of disturbances on the SoC estimation. The key contributions of the proposed algorithm are summarized as:
•
In the presented method, the battery model is derived in the form of a combination of linear and nonlinear parts, and model uncertainties are considered as unknown terms, the effect of which is removed by an unknown input observer.
•
The proposed AHUIO can estimate the sensor faults and eliminate their effects on the estimation of SoC.
•
The effects of stochastic disturbances during the battery operation are eliminated using the Hꝏ mechanism.
•
During the stability proof, a special stability condition is considered, and using an adaptive law, the problem is transformed into a LMI problem. The LMI problem is solved using the YALMIP toolbox, and the estimator parameters are designed in such a way the asymptotic stability and Hꝏ criteria are guaranteed.

The remained sections of this study are categorized as follows: the model of the battery is formulated in section 2. The description of the proposed estimation method is presented in section 3. Section 4 presents the experimental tests for the proposed method together with the battery model identification, and in section 5, the conclusions are provided.

Among the most important modeling methods for lithium-ion batteries such as electrical, electrochemical, and physics-based methods, the electrical models especially, equivalent circuit models (ECM) are the best choices from the accuracy and simplicity points of view. This paper uses the ECM topology with a single RC loop [13] for the battery modeling as is indicated in Fig. 1 . The SoC model is represented by the left network, and the right part in Fig. 1 simulates the transient responses. In this model, R0 and C0 represent the self-discharge and total capacity of the battery. In other words, even if the battery is not connected to any load, its SoC decreases over time, which is caused by self-discharge. Also, every battery has an internal resistance, which is shown by Rin. To simulate the transient response of the battery and include the electrochemical reactions of the battery in the model, the RC network

A single time constant in the RC network captures the time constant of the battery’s response. It has been assumed that the battery has a uniform operation across the cell. Additionally, the equivalent circuit parameters of the battery change for various reasons, such as aging or temperature, leading to uncertainties in the model. Moreover, a fault in the battery current measurement sensor may occur. Therefore, Eq. (11) is modified as follows:

ce:enunciation.ce:label: Remark 1
ce:enunciation.ce:para: From condition (2), it is inferred that the parameter γ plays an important role. This parameter limits the gain of the transfer function between the disturbance signal and the estimation error signal. Therefore, the smaller γ , the less the impact of disturbances on the estimation error and the improved controller performance. On the other hand, parameter γ cannot be smaller than a certain limit because it leads to the infeasibility of the observer problem. Hence, it is necessary to find the minimum value of γ .

ce:section.ce:para: The objective of this paper is to extract the observer gains in Eq. (14) in such a way that the estimation error dynamic in Eq. (23) meets the conditions (1) and (2), and also the value of γ is minimized.

Therefore, the designer must make a compromise when selecting these coefficients.

ce:enunciation.ce:label: Remark 3
ce:enunciation.ce:para:
In general, in the proposed design, the parameters d , c and
Ψ
must be tuned by the designer. The parameters c and
Ψ
are related to the rate of change of the nonlinear term α, which was explained in Remark 2. The parameter d is related to Lemma 2 and is used to overcome the nonlinear terms present in the stability criterion. On the other hand, the parameters related to the UIO (
P 1
,
P 2
,
P 3
, and
P 4
) and the candidate Lyapunov function (
Λ
) are obtained by solving the optimization problem in Eq. (30) and using Eqs. (18)-(21). These parameters do not require tuning by the designer.

ce:enunciation.ce:label: Remark 4
ce:enunciation.ce:para: The optimization problem in Eq. (30) includes several unknown variables, one inequality constraint, and one optimization variable. To solve this problem, the unknown variables are first defined using the “sdpvar” command available in the YALMIP toolbox in MATLAB, and then the inequality constraint is coded. Finally, the optimization problem is solved using the “solvesdp” command, which takes the inequality constraint and the optimization variable as its arguments, and the values of the unknown variables are obtained.

ce:section.ce:para: The conceptual visualization of the suggested estimator is indicated in Fig. 2 , and its implementation algorithm is provided in Table 1 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: The test setup for the experiments
ce:section.ce:para: Using a setup shown in Fig. 3 , a series of experimental tests has been performed for model identification of battery cell, and also, to check the performance of the AHUIO. In this study, a typical 2.4 Ah lithium ion battery, with maximum, nominal, and cut-off voltages of 4.1 V, 3.7 V, and 3 V, respectively, has been used. It is clear in the figure that a liquid crystal display (LCD), voltage and current sensors, a microcontroller, and a programmable resistive load are used in the main circuit of this test bench. This microcontroller controls the load in case of a discharge and is provided with internal 10-bit analogue-to-digital converters for the voltage and current measurement. These data are sent to the computer using an RS232 (serial) connection. The rate of sampling is 20 Hz. Voltage and current signals are utilized both for model identification and model validation purposes.

The experiments have been conducted at room temperature and using the programmable resistive-load, the battery has been discharged to extract the OCV-SoC curve, terminal voltage, and current. In Fig. 4 , the OCV-SoC profile of the purposed fresh battery cell is shown. The operation stops when the terminal voltage in the discharge state reaches the cut-off voltage. The obtained data are used in the proposed method in [18] to extract the battery parameters.

The value of C0 is obtained from the
C 0
= 3600 ×
( c a p a c i t y )
A h
relation. When the calculated SoC based on Ah counting method and based on Eq. (2) are consistent, the extracted self-discharge resistance R0 is correct. The value of Rin , is obtained based on the ratio of voltage to current at the jump moments in their signals [18].

and based on method presented in [7] the values of resistant and capacitor in the RC network are identified. Table 2 reports the parameters in the model of the battery #1.

Since the model uncertainties have been considered as the unknown part of the battery model during the AHUIO design, the robustness against model uncertainty is guaranteed. In Fig. 5 , the voltage from experiments is compared to the voltage from simulated model. As is indicated, the error value is less than 48 mV with an average of about 6.5 mV. This small amount of modeling error shows that the identification accuracy is satisfactory compared to other modeling errors presented in [6].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Experiments for a fresh battery cell
ce:section.ce:para: At the first step of the performance verification, the purposed fresh battery cell was discharged using the programmable resistive load. This resistive load has been programmed so that the battery terminal current becomes a pulse signal with a period of 200 seconds and a bandwidth of 30 %. To check the effectiveness of the proposed observer, its performance for the SoC and terminal voltage estimation has been compared to the conventional SMO. In other words, the AHUIO's purpose is to eliminate the effect of model uncertainties and disturbances and increase the estimation accuracy. So, its performance should be compared to a robust observer. Fig. 6 indicates the determined SoC using both methods. As is obvious in this figure, the AHUIO has an error of less than 0.2 % with no chattering while using the SMO, this error is about 1 % in addition to chattering. Fig. 7 , which indicates the SoC estimation error, confirms the above-mentioned explanations and clarifies the superiority of the AHUIO method over the SMO. During the experiments, the measurement noises have some effects on the estimation process, which is not considered in the conventional SMO. At the same time, the proposed observer has taken into account these effects to eliminate them during the SoC estimation. Moreover, unlike the conventional SMO, the proposed estimator has no chattering in its performance, which causes better accuracy in the estimation. Besides, in the SMO family design, the extracted observe gain through the pole-placement method may not be the best value due to inaccuracies in the trial and failure efforts. While the observer gain of the proposed observer is calculated through the LMI method and using the YALMIP tool. This is another superiority of the presented approach over the SMO, and consequently, the reason for the better performance of the proposed AHUIO.

The output of the battery model (terminal voltage) can be directly measured. So, it is another important metric for the performance evaluation of the observers. Fig. 8 compares the estimated terminal voltage using both methods. As is obvious from this figure and also Fig. 9 , the proposed method has a better performance than the SMO. More precisely, AHUIO has a lower error of 0.15 V. It is worth mentioning that the conventional SMO has a good performance for the terminal voltage estimation, but it has chattering phenomena in its performance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Experiments for an aged battery cell with an uncertain model
ce:section.ce:para: To verify the performance of the AHUIO estimator for an aged battery cell with more model uncertainties, a set of experiments has been carried out for another battery cell with changed model parameters. The battery #2 model parameters have been collected in Table 3 .

For this case, the terminal voltage and SoC have been estimated using the AHUIO and conventional SMO, as is shown in Figs 10 to 13. According to Fig. 10, the AHUIO can has an SoC estimation error of 0.22 % while, this error is about 2.5 % using the SMO. In addition, the chattering of the SMO in not occurred by the AHUIO. The effectiveness of the presented method for the SoC estimation is also confirmed by Fig. 11 . Similar to the previous section, the estimated terminal voltage using both methods has been also investigated. As indicated in Figs. 12 and 13
, the chattering problem and inaccuracy issues have been not indicated by the proposed AHUIO unlike the SMO. According to these figures, the error of terminal voltage estimation by the proposed estimator is less than the conventional SMO as much as 0.2 V. According to the figures related to the aged battery cell, the performances of the two methods have been decreased which is logical. In other words, there are differences between the model parameters of the two battery models. While two observers have been designed for the first battery model. Therefore, their performance will decrease for the second battery. In this case, what matters is the robustness of the proposed observer against increasing the model uncertainty that is created by aging or changing the battery cells. More precisely, it can be noted that the aging phenomenon in lithium-ion batteries directly affects its model parameters and causes a sharp increase in model uncertainty. This increase in model uncertainty naturally requires a more robust method for the SoC estimation to eliminate the effect of aging phenomena on the estimation. As shown in the results obtained in this section, the proposed method has been able to resist this uncertain increase well and maintain its accuracy in estimating the SoC.

Modeling errors or model uncertainties are undeniable factors during the modelling of a battery cell. In other words, during the model parameter identification, there are always some errors that cause model uncertainties. To overcome this issue, this uncertainty has been considered as an additive term (∆) to the overall model of the battery indicated in Eqs. (6) and (11). In this regard, the model uncertainties were considered during the design of the proposed observer, and consequently, the proposed observer was robust against the modeling error. Also, the aging phenomenon directly causes an increase in the model uncertainty (modeling error) and variations in battery capacity over time. In this regard, in this section, the performance of the proposed observer has been checked for an aged battery cell with a larger modeling error compared to a fresh battery cell, and the results confirmed the robust performance of the proposed method and its low sensitivity to the modeling error and variations in battery capacity.

Generally, Fig. 14 compares the performance of the proposed method and SMO in the experimental tests for fresh and aged battery cells. In this figure, the estimation error and convergence time of both methods have been recorded for the SoC and terminal voltage estimation. As is clear, the superiority of the proposed observer is completely obvious based on the values indicated in this figure.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Simulation and experimental results for the sensor fault estimation and elimination
ce:section.ce:para: A series of simulations have been performed to validate the proposed method's performance in sensor fault estimation. In this scenario, a constant fault has been added to the battery current to see how the proposed AHUIO estimates the sensor fault and how the AHUIO eliminates the fault effects on the SoC estimation. The results of this simulation are shown in Figs. 14 and 15 . In this scenario, a fault has been added in the form of a step function to the battery current at time 2020 sec with a final value of 0.25 A. It is worth mentioning that in this simulation, the current profile is a pulse signal with the period and pulse widths of 500 sec and 20 %, respectively.

As is clear from Fig. 15, the AHUIO estimates the sensor fault with an error of 0.02 A. Also, the estimated fault using the proposed observer converges to the true value in less than 3 seconds. In Fig. 16 , the effect of the occurred fault on both proposed AHUIO and conventional SMO has been compared. As is obvious, the SMO cannot converge the true SoC after the fault occurrence. While the proposed AHUIO can eliminate the fault effect on the SoC estimation. To be more specific, AHUIO estimates the occurred fault in 3 seconds and consequently, uses it to estimate the SoC with no fault effects. As is clear from Fig. 16, the AHUIO has a jump at the time of fault occurrence, and after 3 seconds (fault estimation), re-converges to the true SoC (actual value of SoC).

To be clearer, the input information of both observers for the SoC estimation is faulty. Consequently, the SMO, which has no fault estimation, estimates the SoC with inaccurate information and cannot converge to the actual SoC anymore. In contrast, the proposed AHUIO estimates the fault in 3 seconds and estimates the SoC with no effect of the fault.

In order to evaluate the performance of the proposed observer under significant measurement errors, a series of additional experimental tests has been performed in which a constant fault as much as 0.5 A (significant measurement errors) is added to the current measurement sensor at time 6000 sec. The results of this experiment is shown in the following figure.

As is clearly obvious in this figure, even in the presence of significant measurement errors, the proposed observer maintains its accurate performance. According to this figure, SMO has a significant error of 15 % or the SoC estimation. On the other hand, the proposed AHUIO is able to estimate the SoC with an error of less than 0.5 %. The reason for this robustness is the active error elimination of the proposed observer. To be more specific, this error is estimated (regardless of its size) and is eliminated during the estimation of other state variables, including SoC.

The superiority of the AHUIO from robustness, accuracy, and computational cost points of view can be explained as follows:
A-
Robustness: In practical implementations, the robustness of the SoC estimator is an important issue due to the undeniable model uncertainties, measurement faults, and disturbances. The existing robust observers can handle this issue up to a bounded limitation. In addition, the conventional methods have no option for eliminating the effect of measurement faults on the estimation. For this Robustness issue, this paper offers a reliable solution. To be more specific, AHUIO is able to resist model uncertainties and disturbances during the estimation with two separate mechanisms. In other words, using an unknown input observer, the model uncertainties, which are considered as an unknown additive term to the battery model, are handled. Also, using an Hꝏ mechanism, the effect of the disturbances on the SoC estimation is eliminated. Therefore, it can be claimed that AHUIO has a stronger robust performance compared to the other conventional methods.
B-
Accuracy: In line with the previous explanations, the robust observer is able to estimate the sensor faults and eliminate the effect of faults on the SoC estimation. Besides, the proposed AHUIO is able to have a more robust performance against model uncertainties and disturbances compared to the conventional methods (proved by simulation and experimental results). Therefore, the estimation accuracy of the proposed method is better than the conventional algorithms.
C-
Computational cost: Some of conventional robust methods, such as Hꝏ observers, may have heavy computational costs and complexities. In this regard Table compares the conventional methods with the proposed AHUIO from the computational complexity point of view.
Table 4 compares the computational complexities between the proposed method and other methods introduced in the literature.
To investigate the computational time of both proposed methods and SMO, the run time of both methods was measured during the simulations. These times are collected in the following Table 5 .

To check the performance of the proposed observer for the SoC estimation, a series of experimental tests have been done under two different temperatures (40°C and -5°C). In these experimental tests, the battery cell used in section 4.3 is used.

The result of these experiments is shown in Fig. 18 . As is clear from this figure, the proposed observer is able to keep its good performance under the temperature variation condition. To be more specific, changing the temperature causes model uncertainties. In the presence of these uncertainties, the robustness of the observer plays an important role. According to this figure, the proposed observer has an estimation error of 0.2 % at different temperatures. On the other hand, the SMO has an error of 2.5 % in this condition. In addition, the chattering problem is completely obvious in these tests.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Experimental tests for comparison to Kalman filters
ce:section.ce:para: To better compare the proposed method to other existing algorithms, a series of additional experiments has been performed (for the aged battery cell), and the performance of the proposed method has been compared to an Adaptive Extended Kalman Filter (AEKF) [42]. The result of this experimental test is indicated in Fig. 19 :

As can be seen in this figure, the proposed AHUIO has still been able to show much better performance than the AEKF method in the presence of sensor faults. In these experiments, the AEKF method has an estimation error of 5 % (in steady state) in the SoC estimation. Since Kalman filters require accurate information from the battery model, in addition to the fault that has occurred, model uncertainties and disturbances have also reduced the performance of AEKF.
To give a better presentation of the experimental results, the root mean square error (RMSE) of SoC estimation is collected in the Table 6 .

Accuracy and robustness against model and input uncertainties as well as disturbances and measurement faults are the most important challenges during the design of SoC observers for lithium-ion batteries. This paper addressed these issues and presented an AHUIO observer to estimate the SoC with an accurate and robust estimation performance. First, the battery model was derived in the form of a combination of linear and nonlinear parts, and the effect of model uncertainties was removed using an unknown input observer. Then, the problem of stochastic disturbances during the battery operation was solved using the Hꝏ mechanism. Furthermore, by solving an LMI problem using the YALMIP toolbox, the estimator parameters were designed in such a way the asymptotic stability and Hꝏ criteria were guaranteed. Besides, the proposed AHUIO observer was able to estimate the sensor faults and eliminate their effects on the SoC estimation. A set of experimental tests was conducted to validate the effectiveness of the proposed method for fresh and aged battery cells. According to the experimental results, the proposed method indicated an estimation error of 0.2 % and 0.0.42 % for fresh and aged cells.
